original_abstract: The African Independent Churches Association (AICA) represents a distinct chapter in the evolution of South African theological education. While the association initially established its own Theological College, it eventually sought a strategic partnership with the Umphumulo Lutheran Theological College (ULTC) to facilitate student training. This transition highlights a profound concern within African Independent Churches (AICs) regarding the formal education of their leadership. Driven by a mission to preserve doctrinal integrity while ensuring that theological training remains deeply rooted in the lived experiences of their congregants, these institutions view pastoral education as a vital tool for community service. This article investigates the motivations behind the AICs' decision to utilize the ULTC for theological development. Specifically, it examines the early partnerships between these institutions and explores why the AICs moved toward training students within mainline South African theological frameworks. Furthermore, the study evaluates the successes and challenges faced by the AICA Theological College, concluding with an analysis of curriculum limitations and the subsequent support initiatives provided by the Lutheran church.
humanized_abstract: The African Independent Churches Association (AICA) constitutes a new development in South African theological education. The AICA decided on their own Theological College initially, but later decided on a partnership with the Umphumulo Lutheran Theological College (ULTC) for training students at the college. This decision by the AICs to train their pastors through the ULTC, was driven by a mission to ensure that pastoral education strengthens the doctrine of the Church, while it is contemporary and relevant to the experiences of the people in the Church. They regard pastoral education as a service to the community. This article will explore the reason for the partnerships between the AICs and the mainstream South African theological education institutions, the experiences of the AICA Theological College, and will finally conclude with the reasons for the curriculum problems experienced at the college, and the remedies provided by the Lutheran church.

They regard pastoral education as a service to the community.
